title: Modals
intro: Every modal maps to one of four width tiers, one internal spacing rhythm, and one footer layout. The content varies; the frame never does.

items.rule: Pick the smallest named tier that fits: sm 480px, md 640px, lg 800px, xl 1080px — never an arbitrary width.
items.why: Widths 20-40px apart are visually indistinguishable, but every one-off (500px, 520px) becomes another variant to maintain. Content either fits a tier or it needs the next one up.

items.rule: Every modal is three zones — header, body, footer — in that order.
items.why: A fixed anatomy lets users parse any modal instantly: the title says where they are, the footer says how they leave.

items.rule: Body padding is 24px on all sides; fields sit 16px apart; logical sections sit 24px apart.
items.why: This matches the page-level form rhythm, so content moved into a modal keeps its proportions.

items.rule: Footer buttons right-align in a flex row with an 8px gap — secondary first, primary (theme1) last.
items.why: Bottom-right is where users expect the affirmative action; an identical footer in every modal builds muscle memory.
Two themed buttons split attention and stall the exact decision the modal exists to force.

items.rule: Drive visibility with `open`, reset state with `destroyOnHidden`, listen with `afterOpenChange` — never the antd v4 names (`visible`, `destroyOnClose`, `afterVisibleChange`).
items.why: antd v5 keeps `visible` and `destroyOnClose` only as deprecated fallbacks (`open ?? visible`, `destroyOnHidden ?? destroyOnClose`) that log dev-console warnings and lose whenever the v5 prop is also set; `afterVisibleChange` is removed from Modal entirely. The repo CLAUDE.md mistakes table bans the v4 names outright.

items.rule: Submit forms from the footer with `onClick={() => form.submit()}`, never `htmlType='submit'`.
items.why: The footer renders outside the <Form> element in the DOM, so native submit never fires.

items.rule: Modals are for blocking decisions; reach for a Drawer or inline panel when users need the page behind.
items.why: The mask removes all context. If the task requires referencing background content or scrolling past a viewport, the modal is fighting the user.

blocks.title: Size tiers
blocks.caption: The four named width tiers at relative scale — sm 480, md 640, lg 800, xl 1080 — with the use case each tier exists for. The jumps between tiers are deliberate: content fits or it moves up a tier.

blocks.title: Internal spacing zones
blocks.caption: The body rhythm: 24px padding on all sides, 16px between fields within a section, 24px between sections. One annotated wireframe covers every spacing decision inside a modal body.

blocks.title: Standard footer
blocks.caption: The only footer layout: flex row, justify-content flex-end, 8px gap, secondary (no theme) then primary (theme1). Secondary first in DOM, primary last.

blocks.title: Full modal anatomy
blocks.caption: The canonical three-zone structure assembled: header title bar, 24px-padded body with 16px field gaps and a 24px section gap, right-aligned footer with 8px button gap.

blocks.title: Do's and don'ts
blocks.caption: Named tiers vs arbitrary widths, and the standard footer vs mixed space-between footers — the two ways modals most often drift off pattern.

blocks.title: Modal Tokens
blocks.intro: Four width tiers plus four internal spacing tokens. Every dimension in a modal comes from this table.

rows.use: Body content area padding, all sides
Gap between form fields within a section
Gap between logical sections in the body

blocks.title: Canonical Modal Skeleton
blocks.note: Width is a tier value, never a guess. The explicit footer override locks in secondary-then-primary order and the 8px gap; `form.submit()` from onClick is required because the footer renders outside the <Form> DOM.

items.do: Map every modal to a named tier — sm 480, md 640, lg 800, xl 1080 — and pick the smallest that fits.
items.dont: Invent widths like 500px or 520px because the content almost fits.
items.why: Tiers 20-40px apart look identical on screen but multiply variants in code. If content doesn't fit, jump a full tier.

items.do: Right-align footer buttons at an 8px gap: Cancel, then the single theme1 primary.
items.dont: Use space-between footers, 16px button gaps, or per-modal button arrangements.
items.why: One footer layout across the app means users never hunt for the confirm button.

items.do: Lay out body content with Vertical gap={16} inside sections and 24px between sections.
items.why: Tokenized gaps survive refactors; ad-hoc margins drift modal by modal.

items.title: antd v5: `open`, not `visible`
items.detail: antd v5 keeps `visible` only as a deprecated fallback — Modal resolves `open ?? visible`, logs a dev-console deprecation warning, and ignores `visible` entirely once `open` is present. Write open={isOpen}; the repo CLAUDE.md mistakes table bans `visible`.

items.title: antd v5: `destroyOnHidden`, not `destroyOnClose`
items.detail: antd v5 resolves `destroyOnHidden ?? destroyOnClose`, so the v4 name still works but logs a deprecation warning and is banned by the repo CLAUDE.md mistakes table. Without destroyOnHidden, form state survives close and the next open shows stale values.

items.title: antd v5: `onOpenChange`, not `onVisibleChange`
items.detail: Applies to the open-state surfaces around a modal (Dropdown, Popover, Tooltip) — Modal itself listens with `afterOpenChange`. In antd v5 the v4 callback still fires as a deprecated alias with a console warning; write all handlers against onOpenChange.

items.title: GraviButton renders `buttonText`, not children
items.detail: A footer written as <GraviButton>Cancel</GraviButton> renders an empty button. Self-close and pass buttonText='Cancel'.

blocks.title: Choosing the Surface and the Tier
blocks.body:
Reach for a modal when the user must make a focused, blocking decision — confirm a delete, fill a short form, resolve a conflict — and the page behind is irrelevant until they do. When the user needs to reference the page behind, or the content scrolls past a viewport, use a Drawer or an inline panel instead (see Panels).

Tier choice is a content question, not a taste question: count fields and sections. One to three fields is `sm`; a standard single-section form is `md`; a multi-section form is `lg`; anything embedding a grid is `xl` — and a grid inside a modal is itself a signal to consider a dedicated page.

`Modal.confirm` and its siblings are fine for one-line confirmations with no inputs. The moment a modal collects data, it gets the full three-zone layout: titled header, 24px-padded body, standard footer.
